answers: Short summary of the problem - External users see "502 - Bad Gateway" while logins from the internal network work fine. The same FQDN fails but a direct (alternate) path to the reverse proxy behaves differently (works) [Data: Sources (15)]. - Tests showed the header X-Forwarded-Proto was missing on external requests. When TLS inspection on the WAF was temporarily disabled, external logins worked; re-enabling inspection produced 502 again [Data: Sources (15)]. - After a security/firewall patch the WAF/reverse-proxy configuration (inspection template and custom header rewrite rule) had been reset/overwritten which caused the backend to no longer see HTTPS semantics it expects [Data: Sources (15, 17)]. What is the likely root cause - The most likely root cause is a change to the WAF / reverse-proxy configuration after a security patch that removed or reverted the custom header-rewrite rules and replaced the portal-specific template with a generic template. That caused TLS inspection to strip or fail to forward the X-Forwarded-Proto header, so the backend (which expects HTTPS signalling) returned 502 for external requests via the FQDN [Data: Sources (15, 17)]. - This conclusion matches the ticket investigation and closure: inspection/TLS termination behavior plus missing header rewrite rules after a patch, and restoration of the documented blueprint configuration fixed the issue [Data: Sources (15, 17)]. Should this be handled by 1st-level or 2nd-level support? - This is a 2nd-level (Network / Security / WAF / Reverse Proxy) issue. The ticket in the example was escalated from helpdesk to the BU Network & Security and taken over by a system engineer focusing on WAF/Reverse Proxy configuration [Data: Sources (15)]. The problem requires changes to WAF templates, inspection policies and header rewrite rules - tasks outside typical 1st-level scope [Data: Sources (17)]. Recommended immediate checks and actions (in priority order) 1. Reproduce and confirm the behavior - Verify external access produces 502 while internal access works and direct-IP tests behave differently (this was already observed and is a useful diagnostic) [Data: Sources (15)]. 2. Quick confirmation step (safe test) - Temporarily disable TLS inspection on the WAF for the portal service to confirm whether the header/inspection is the cause (in the incident disabling TLS inspection made logins work) [Data: Sources (15)]. 3. Inspect WAF / Reverse Proxy configuration - Check which WAF template/profile is active for the portal: ensure the portal-specific template (Portal-App-Template) is used rather than a generic Default-Strict profile [Data: Sources (15, 17)]. - Verify the custom header rewrite rule exists and that X-Forwarded-Proto: https and X-Forwarded-Host are set and not removed by inspection [Data: Sources (15, 17)]. 4. Restore documented blueprint configuration - If the template or rewrite rule was overwritten by a patch, reapply the blueprint's documented settings (Portal-App-Template, header rewrite, inspection template exceptions) and re-run external tests [Data: Sources (15, 17)]. 5. Re-enable inspection and validate - Re-enable TLS inspection only after header rewrite / exceptions are in place and validate end-to-end. Monitor for at least 24 hours for stability as done in the example ticket [Data: Sources (15)]. 6. Post-patch validation process - Add a checklist to validate the WAF template, custom rewrite rules and inspection policy after future security/firmware patches (this is explicitly recommended in the blueprint) [Data: Sources (17)]. Notes on communication & priority - Treat this as high priority (customers affected) and notify the Network & Security team; the example incident was classified as High and escalated accordingly [Data: Sources (15)]. - When making changes, document the exact template, header rule and inspection adjustments so a future patch can be validated against the blueprint and the change can be audited [Data: Sources (17)]. If you want, I can: - Draft the exact checklist and commands/settings to verify the WAF template and header-rewrite rules (if you tell me the WAF vendor/model), or - Draft a short escalation email to your Network & Security / WAF team summarizing the required checks and recommended temporary mitigation. Which would you prefer?
